properties.name: Golden Gate Crossing — Borrowed Launches
properties.summary: 32 horses ferried across the Golden Gate using launches borrowed from visiting San Blas frigates Princesa and Aranzazu. Spain cannot cross its own bay without foreign ships.
properties.result: Spain depends on visiting vessels to cross its own frontier

properties.name: Goycoechea's Overland Expedition to Bodega
properties.summary: 10 soldiers + sergeant march overland from SF via Bolinas and Tomales Bay to Bodega. Coast Miwok direct them to harder western route — later admitted "deceived." Territory 45 miles north of SF is "tierra incognita."
properties.result: "Tierra incognita" — Spain's own governor admits unknown territory

properties.name: Moraga's 1st Interior Expedition
properties.summary: First major Central Valley expedition. Named the Sacramento River. 30+ stops, 17 villages contacted. No permanent occupation.
properties.result: Reconnaissance only — a round trip

properties.name: Moraga's 2nd Expedition — Sacramento Valley
properties.summary: Second interior campaign. Again, a round trip.

properties.name: Battle of Suisun
properties.summary: 17 men cross Carquinez Strait by boat to attack Suisun ranchería. Suisun had killed 16 Christians over prior 3 years. Hard fight — survivors in huts preferred to "perish in the flames" rather than surrender. Moraga received royal commendations.
properties.result: Spanish tactical victory at the frontier chokepoint

quote.en: "Es mejor morir libres que vivir como esclavos."

properties.name: Moraga Investigates Russian Bodega
properties.summary: Moraga goes by boat to Bodega. Finds 40+ Aleut hunting canoes, Russian supply ships at anchor. Reconnaissance, no confrontation. Protests to commandant.
properties.result: Round trip — reconnaissance only

properties.name: Moraga Discovers Fort Ross
properties.summary: Moraga with 4 soldiers investigates grounded vessel. Discovers Fort Ross: ~80 men, Kuskov commanding, all from Unalaska/Kamchatka. Vessel 8 leagues above Bodega. No opposition.
properties.result: Discovery — no confrontation possible

properties.name: Moraga's Fort Ross Defense Assessment
properties.summary: 40 Russians, 80 Aleuts, 12 guns (4-pounder), 3 falconets. Spanish troops: "no shirts, no pants, no hats." The most damning comparison in the archive.
properties.result: Russians armed and supplied; Spanish naked

properties.name: Argüello's Northern Expedition
properties.summary: 55 men — one of the largest Spanish/Mexican expeditions in CA history. Two full days spent ferrying horses across Carquinez Strait with 4 assembled boats. A power that needs a weekend to cross a channel does not control the territory beyond it.
properties.result: Two days to cross the Strait — not an empire moving through its own territory

properties.name: Colnett's Argonaut at Bodega
properties.summary: British ship takes refuge during storm — cuts spars, makes repairs. Later misidentified by Spanish as active English threat. Ghost presence that haunted Spanish intelligence for years.

properties.name: Vancouver's 1st Visit to San Francisco
properties.summary: HMS Discovery and Chatham anchor at SF. Warm reception from Commandant Sal. Menzies walks to Fort Point — finds one cannon "lash'd to a log of Wood." British see the weakness firsthand.
properties.result: British assess SF defenses — one burst cannon

properties.name: English at Bodega "con los cañones en tierra"
properties.summary: Arrillaga reports an English vessel anchored at Bodega with "cannons on land." Identity uncertain — Colnett traces? New vessel? Rumor? Same year as Spanish Bodega failure.
properties.result: Foreign military presence in territory Spain cannot reach

properties.name: Menzies Lands at Tomales Bay
properties.summary: Menzies lands from Vancouver's ship. Finds cross made from cask stave, horse tracks from Goycoechea's expedition. Coast Miwok calling "Amico," speaking Spanish, pointing up lagoon repeating "Spaniard." British find Spanish traces in territory Spain couldn't hold.
properties.result: British find evidence of failed Spanish expedition

properties.name: Vancouver's 2nd Visit — Concealment Policy
properties.summary: Dramatic change from 1st visit. Armed guards, 2-mile limit, refused access to mission. But 8 new brass 4-pounders visible on beach, workers building battery. Spain scrambles to hide weakness and show strength simultaneously.
properties.result: Concealment policy — Spain hides its defenses from the one power that matters

properties.name: Zúñiga Shows Bodega Plans to British — No Concealment
properties.summary: San Diego commandant Zúñiga freely shows Bodega harbor plans to British officers. No concealment at all — contradicts the tight security at SF. The policy was inconsistent.
properties.result: Concealment policy fails at San Diego

properties.name: English Launches at Santa Cruz
properties.summary: English launches spotted off Santa Cruz coast, withdrew. Sgt. Pedro Amador sent as reinforcement. Soldier Mesa punished for misreporting.
properties.result: English probe — withdrew

properties.name: English Shipwreck at Point Reyes / Farallones
properties.summary: Ship sinks near Farallones. Indigenous informants report two uniformed Englishmen at Point Reyes. English naval presence at the Bay entrance during wartime. The report comes through indigenous intelligence networks.
properties.result: English at the Bay entrance — indigenous networks provide the warning

properties.name: English Vessel at Santa Cruz
properties.summary: English ship arrives at Santa Cruz port, 60 miles from SF Bay. Vallejo coordinates with alcalde — no military officer available. Thin defense exposed.
properties.result: No military response possible

properties.name: English Corsair Campaign — Gulf of California
properties.summary: 4 English frigates (22–24 guns each) raided Gulf of California shipping. Ransomed ships, captured brigs. Governor Arrillaga: "sin ningunos elementos de defensa." Maritime reinforcement route to California contested.
properties.result: English naval dominance — supply route threatened

properties.name: HMS Raccoon at San Francisco
properties.summary: British warship from Columbia River. HMS Raccoon had seized Fort Astoria Dec 1813. Needed gunpowder. Crew deserting to Spanish protection as "Roman Catholics." Written in English in the archive.
properties.result: British warship uses SF as supply port — War of 1812 reaches Pacific

properties.name: Eliza's Activo at Bodega — Spain's Last Pacific Exploration
properties.summary: Eliza arrives in Activo after near-starvation NW Coast voyage. Spain's last Pacific exploration. The exhausted vessel barely makes it to Bodega — the empire's reach is collapsing.
properties.result: Last Spanish Pacific exploration — barely survived

properties.name: Rezanov Arrives on Juno
properties.summary: Nikolai Rezanov — Imperial Chamberlain, RAC shareholder, diplomat. Arrives at SF. Concepción Argüello courtship. Intelligence gathering on Spanish defenses. First Russian official at SF.
properties.result: Russia assesses Spanish California firsthand

properties.name: RAC Fur-Collecting Begins at Bodega
properties.summary: 130 Aleut hunters aboard Kodiak. First Russian representative: Tarakanov. 40+ Russians + 150 Indians by 1809 (Argüello report). Moraga protests — ignored.
properties.result: Permanent Russian presence established — Spain protests but cannot act

properties.name: 130 Bidarkas in San Francisco Bay
properties.summary: 130 Aleut bidarkas (kayaks) hunting sea otter inside San Francisco Bay. The Russians are inside Spain's premier port and Spain cannot stop them.
properties.result: Russian hunting inside the Bay — sovereignty violated

properties.name: Farallon Islands — Russian Sealing Station
properties.summary: Russian sealing station on the Farallones — 18 miles off the coast of San Francisco. Aleut hunters camped seasonally. Spain/Mexico never contested it.
properties.result: Russian territory visible from SF — unchallenged

properties.name: Kotzebue Visits on Rurik
properties.summary: Otto von Kotzebue arrives at SF on scientific circumnavigation. Spain demands Ross abandonment. Diplomatic failure — Russia ignores the demand.
properties.result: Spanish diplomatic protest — Russia ignores it

properties.name: Golovnin on Kamchatka at SF
properties.summary: Captain Golovnin arrives at SF on world circumnavigation aboard Kamchatka. Same season as Russian Kutuzov and American Ontario. Three-power naval convergence in one month.
properties.result: Three-power convergence: Russia + Russia + America in SF at once

properties.name: Russian Kutuzov at SF
properties.summary: RAC vessel Kutuzov at SF alongside Golovnin's warship and American Ontario. Spanish garrison watches foreign warships parade through their port.

properties.name: Vasiliev Corvettes at SF
properties.summary: Two Russian warships anchor at SF — sailing past the fortress that had 6 functional cannons and no grapeshot. Argüello follows orders to receive them "cordially and hospitably."
properties.result: Russian warships pass indefensible fortress — received as guests

properties.name: Hagemeister Treaty with Kashaya Pomo
properties.summary: Russia negotiates land cession with Kashaya Pomo chiefs — not Spanish authorities. "Allies of Russia" medals distributed 1819. Russians treat indigenous peoples as sovereign landowners because, north of the Bay, they are.
properties.result: Russia treats Pomo as sovereign — Spain does not exist here

properties.name: Battle of Punta de Guijarros — Lelia Byrd
properties.summary: First naval engagement between a Spanish battery and foreign vessel. American smuggling + armed rescue + artillery duel. Shaler's crew put pistols to Spanish guards' chests. Ship escaped under grapeshot fire. Spanish soldiers were complicit in the otter skin trade.
properties.result: First foreign attack on California — American smugglers

properties.name: American Frigate at SF — Troop Crisis
properties.summary: Damaged American frigate anchors in SF port. Argüello forced to pull invalidos (retired soldiers) from San José to augment escorts. Chronic troop shortage: presidio can't staff itself when a foreign ship appears.
properties.result: One foreign ship exposes garrison collapse

properties.name: USS Ontario at San Francisco
properties.summary: American warship Ontario at SF — same month as Golovnin's Kamchatka and RAC Kutuzov. Three nations' vessels in the Bay simultaneously. Spain watches.
properties.result: American warship in the Bay

properties.name: Mercury Trial — Capt. Eyres
properties.summary: Boston captain, 8 years trading with Russians and NW Coast Indians. Testimony at Santa Barbara. American commercial penetration of the coast — trading network connecting Boston, Russian Alaska, and California.
properties.result: American-Russian commercial network exposed

properties.name: Anglo-American Vessel Blockades Guaymas
properties.summary: Brig "Cesak," Captain "Clarick," raised American flag, fired 3 cannon shots, blockaded Guaymas. American aggression on Pacific coast in same period as Bouchard.
properties.result: American naval aggression on Pacific coast

properties.name: Bouchard's Attack on Monterey
properties.summary: Argentine privateer: Argentina (38 guns) + Santa Rosa (26 guns), ~285 crew. 400 men landed. Presidio burned. Only time a California capital fell to a foreign enemy. Solá retreated to Salinas with archives + 6,000 cartridges.
properties.result: MONTEREY FALLS — only foreign capture of a California capital

properties.name: Bouchard at Refugio
properties.summary: Bouchard lands, plunders Ortega's rancho, kills cattle. 3 insurgents captured in ambush: Lt. William Taylor (Boston), Martin Romero (Paraguay), Mateo José Pascual.
properties.result: Ranch plundered; 3 captured by Spanish ambush

properties.name: Bouchard at Santa Barbara — Guerra's Bluff
properties.summary: Bouchard anchors. Guerra bluffs with marching troops trick — soldiers march around a hill repeatedly to look like a large army. Prisoner exchange. No fighting.
properties.result: Bluff succeeds — no attack

properties.name: Bouchard at San Juan Capistrano
properties.summary: Bouchard lands. Minor damage — straw houses burned, wine spilled. 4 insurgent deserters surrender.
properties.result: Minor damage

properties.name: La Pérouse at Monterey
properties.summary: First foreign account of Spanish military capacity. Governor Fages tells La Pérouse "North California has no northern boundary at all — it extends to Mount Saint Elias." Reality: 282 soldiers for 800+ leagues of coast, 25 missions, ~50,000 Indians, only 10,000 Christian. Continental claim vs. actual control — 7 years before Nootka.
properties.result: France sees through the fiction — 282 soldiers for 800 leagues

properties.name: Coast Miwok Intelligence to Sal
properties.summary: Coast Miwok informant tells Commandant Sal two ships at Bodega — soldiers "dressed like ours turned inside out." Earliest warning of foreign presence, 3 weeks before diplomatic intelligence through official channels.
properties.result: Indigenous networks faster than imperial intelligence

properties.name: Coast Miwok Deceive Goycoechea
properties.summary: Coast Miwok tell Goycoechea the eastern shores are "impassable" — he takes the harder western route via Marin headlands. Later admitted he was "deceived." Information control: indigenous peoples directing Spanish military movements.
properties.result: Indigenous information warfare — Spain deceived

properties.name: Coast Miwok Calibrated Diplomacy at Bodega
properties.summary: Coast Miwok "prepared to give us battle" — choose instead to present pinole and fruit. Not submission; calibrated response. They evaluated the Spanish party and chose diplomacy.
properties.result: Military capability held in reserve — diplomacy chosen

properties.name: Coast Miwok Bilingual Intermediaries at Tomales
properties.summary: Coast Miwok calling "Amico," speaking Spanish, pointing up lagoon repeating "Spaniard." Bilingual intermediaries positioning between British and Spanish empires. They know both powers and broker between them.
properties.result: Trilingual positioning between empires

properties.name: Guirmenes Intel on Bodega — "Keep This Indian Happy"
properties.summary: Indigenous informant (Guirmenes) reports no foreign ship at Bodega for 2 years. Governor orders: "previene se tenga á ese indio contento" (keep this Indian happy). Indigenous surveillance network sustained across years — Spain depends on it.
properties.result: Spain pays for indigenous intelligence

properties.name: Suisun Resist Moraga at Carquinez
properties.summary: Suisun warriors fight Moraga at the Strait. Had killed 16 Christians over prior 3 years. This is a military frontier — Spain fights across it, does not govern beyond it.
properties.result: Indigenous military control of the Strait frontier

properties.name: Suispaques Evade Cabo Ortega
properties.summary: Cabo Ortega dispatched to capture Suispaques/Guispaques — returned without success. Indigenous captain later offered to return fugitives himself — on his own terms.
properties.result: Indigenous autonomy — Spanish military cannot operate here

properties.name: Chief Vallí-éla Requests Russian Flag
properties.summary: Chief Vallí-éla of Bodega Miwok requests Russian protection and asks Golovnin for a Russian flag. "Very glad to have Russian neighbors." Three independent accounts (Golovnin, Matiushkin, Lütke). The indigenous chief chooses his empire.
properties.result: Indigenous imperial choice — requests Russian protection

sources.citation: Golovnin 1979:165 via Farris 1998:6; AVPRI deep read lines 176–190.

properties.name: Gualinela's Armed Resistance — 200 Men
properties.summary: Chief Gualinela of Tiutume ranchería (Bodega), 43 people. 200 armed men — heathen + Christian neophytes from Mission San Rafael — band together to resist Mexican troops. Cross-mission alliance.
properties.result: Armed indigenous coalition — 40 years of continuity at Bodega

properties.name: Sal's Boundary Letter — "English Want SF Bay for a Boundary"
properties.summary: Sal to Arrillaga: "Segun sus opiniones, quieren los ingleses la boca de este puerto por línea divisoria." Also reports one burst cannon, two English frigates at Bodega. Intelligence from Bodega y Quadra at Nootka.
properties.result: THESIS-CRITICAL — the boundary claim stated explicitly

properties.name: SF Cannon Burst — Sole Weapon Destroyed
properties.summary: Cannon exploded during saint's day firing. 10 pieces, fragments reached church 125 varas. Fired by Soldado Arriola. San Francisco's sole defensive weapon destroyed before the Nootka crisis peaked.
properties.result: One cannon, and it burst

properties.name: Arrillaga: "Presidios Are Indefensible — English Said So"
properties.summary: "Los Presidios de Alta Cal. están indefensos, cuya circunstancia ha sido notada por los mismos ingleses... no pueden resistir contra una invasion." The governor's own admission to his viceroy.
properties.result: THESIS-CRITICAL — Spain admits its own weakness

properties.name: Arrillaga: "Tierra Incognita"
properties.summary: "El grande rodeo y tierra incognita." Territory 45 miles north of SF is literally unknown to the Spanish governor after 24 years in California.
properties.result: THESIS-CRITICAL — Spain does not know its own territory

sources.citation: bodega-failure-1793; Prov. St. Pap., MS., xxi. 113.

properties.name: Viceroy Orders Bodega — "Whether or Not English Are There"
properties.summary: Revilla Gigedo orders Bodega occupied. Full fortification plan for Bodega + SF + Monterey + SD. Never executed. Supplies eventually repurposed (June 10, 1794).
properties.result: Order given — never carried out

properties.name: Fiscal: "Fortifications Would Not Prevent a Landing"
properties.summary: Crown fiscal officer declares defense should be naval; land fortifications useless. Devastating assessment from within the imperial bureaucracy itself.
properties.result: Spain's own bureaucracy declares California indefensible

properties.name: Yerba Buena Battery Abandoned
properties.summary: Argüello reports Yerba Buena Battery unmanned — only a daily visit by the vigía. SF garrison: 1 cabo, 4 artilleros, 3 cabos, 10 soldados. Barely sufficient for Presidio + fort. The key battery controlling the narrows has no one in it.
properties.result: Key battery unmanned — "imaginary" defense

properties.name: Pedro de Nava: Isolation Was Deliberate Policy
properties.summary: Comandante General of the Provincias Internas explicitly rejects overland California–New Mexico road. Cites 1786 prohibition. California's territories "useless." Indigenous nations form impassable barrier. The only route is the Sea of Cortez.
properties.result: THESIS-CRITICAL — isolation was policy, not accident

properties.name: Fortress San Joaquín in Collapse
properties.summary: Only 8 cannons (2 non-functional), no grapeshot, no forge, no hot-shot furnace, wadding falling apart, cartridges irreplaceable. Two years after Bouchard proved the vulnerability — still not repaired.
properties.result: The fortress is a ruin

properties.name: Solá to Viceroy: San Rafael + Russian Threat
properties.summary: Same letter contains Mission San Rafael founding AND Russian threat warning. Dual purpose: health + strategic. Proves mission expansion was explicitly about territorial defense against Russia.
properties.result: Missions as counter-Russian strategy

properties.name: The 1793 Bodega Occupation Attempt — Matute / Aránzazu
properties.summary: The occupation the whole story turns on. On the Viceroy's order to "reconocer y ocupar dicho Puerto, quitando á extranjeros ese abrigo inmediato á los Presidios," an expedition under Lt. Juan Bautista Matute aboard the Aránzazu was to plant a Spanish establishment at Bodega. Matute reported he "no verificaría aquel año el establecimiento" — the attempt collapsed for want of imperial capacity, not suitable ground.
properties.result: The establishment that did not happen — Spain's claim north of SF never occupied

properties.name: Bodega Establishment Abandoned
properties.summary: Sal reports that "no se efectuará el establecimiento del puerto de Bodega," and that the articles destined for Bodega have been reassigned to the San Francisco presidio. The closing bracket on the 1793 order-and-attempt.
properties.result: The Bodega plan formally dead — supplies redirected to SF

properties.name: Wilcocks Calls at Russian Bodega — and the Annexation Plot
properties.summary: The American trader James Smith Wilcocks calls at "un establecimiento ruso llamado Bodega" — "los rusos no quisieron comprar nada" — then sells Gov. Solá $700 of blankets for the four presidios. In 1817 he is accused of plotting to "levantarme con las Californias para integrarlas a los anglo-americanos" — the earliest dated articulation of the U.S.-annexation fear the open coast invited.
properties.result: Bodega as a (failed) commercial port in the US–Russian–Spanish triangle

sources.citation: C-A 13 Docs 193–206 (the Wilcocks affair; the Bodega call & blanket sale in his voyage account, Doc 206, orig. i.716–726; the annexation accusation denounced in Doc 203). Formerly cited as survey entry 19.

properties.name: Elliot de Castro / Ilmena Seized at Refugio
properties.summary: The Russian brig Ilmena's shore party — John Elliot de Castro, an American, an Aleut and others — is seized at Ortega's Refugio rancho, the coast's otter-smuggling rendezvous. A $2,000 ransom is offered and refused. Spain/Mexico can catch the people but not stop the trade.
properties.result: The Mexican-era sequel — Refugio as a second Bodega-style keyhole

properties.name: RAC Parlamentario — Podushkin on the Chirikoff at Monterey
properties.summary: Baranov, from Novo-Arkhangelsk, protests the "insults to the Russian Flag and the capture and keeping in cruel bondage of Russians… in the waters and the coasts of California," invokes the 1812 treaty, and sends Lt. Podushkin as a parlamentario aboard the Chirikoff to Monterey. Russia negotiating as a commercial sovereign on Spain's claimed coast.
properties.result: Russia treats with Spain as an equal power on the California coast

sources.citation: C-A 13 Docs 263–264 (Podushkin's request, 22 Apr 1817; Baranov's protest, 2 Feb 1817). Formerly cited as survey entry 20.

properties.name: Kuskov's RAC Letters to the SF Commandant
properties.summary: Ivan Kuskov, RAC commercial councillor at Ross, writes directly to the SF commandant "in the name of the Honorable Russian-American Company under special protection of His Majesty the Emperor of all the Russias," sending agent Slobodchikov to settle accounts and pressing for the return of captured Kodiak Aleuts.
properties.result: Ross corresponds with SF as one sovereign to another

properties.name: Spanish Missions Supply Kuskov at Fort Ross
properties.summary: The Spanish missions supplying Kuskov's Russian colony — Spain's own establishments provisioning the foreign outpost on the ground it claimed. The open coast as routine economic fact, running in Spain's disfavor.
properties.result: Spain provisions the colony it was ordered to uproot

properties.name: Fort Ross Abandonment Resolved
properties.summary: The RAC Main Board resolves to abandon Ross and "sell or barter for wheat to the inhabitants of San Francisco." The otter gone, Ross is liquidated — the economic close of the arc that opened with the resource.
properties.result: Russia leaves — Ross sold to Sutter, a promoter of the US invasion

sources.citation: Fort Ross AVPRI, 1841 abandonment resolution (via Gibson & Istomin).

properties.name: Yanovsky's RAC Trade Overture
properties.summary: RAC chief manager Semyon Yanovsky proposes a joint, equal-shares otter-hunt and open commerce (via Khlebnikov and the Madrid embassy; the Ilmena to supply Ross). Solá replies he cannot grant otter-hunting — only the Viceroy can. The third volume of one continuous Russo-Spanish negotiation.
properties.result: Russia again negotiates as a commercial sovereign — Spain defers to Mexico City

properties.name: The Suvorov at SF + the RAC Proclamation to California
properties.summary: The RAC ship Suvorov (Lazarev) calls at San Francisco carrying a formal RAC directors' overture "to the noble and honoured Spanish inhabitants of California," invoking Russo-Spanish friendship, the 1812 treaty, and Rezanov's 1806 visit — the institutional Russian demand for the California provision trade, arriving by ship.
properties.result: The RAC's formal trade-opening request, delivered to SF

sources.citation: C-A 12 C478 + C479 (Suvorov 1815; passport-proclamation St. Petersburg, 12 Jul 1811).

properties.name: British Squadron Sounds San Diego Harbor
properties.summary: A sworn 1799 deposition: a British naval squadron awaiting the Manila galleon, with 22 Peru prizes, states a plan to winter at San Diego — whose harbour they had already secretly sounded c.1797, "entered in their boats one moonlit night, sounded the whole harbour," "instruidos por los planos de Nutka." The open coast as an actual reconnaissance ground.
properties.result: San Diego's harbor already mapped by the enemy — the threat the 1793 order feared

properties.name: Presidio of San Francisco
properties.summary: 1776. Northernmost military post. $1,400 total investment by 1793. Garrison: ~35 men.

properties.name: Fort San Joaquín (Castillo de SF)
properties.summary: 1794. Battery guarding the Golden Gate narrows. 1 cannon (burst 1792), 8 by 1793, 6 functional by 1820. Unmanned by 1800.

properties.name: Presidio of Monterey
properties.summary: 1770. Capital of Alta California. Burned by Bouchard 1818.

properties.name: Presidio of San Diego
properties.summary: 1769. First presidio. Punta de Guijarros battery.

properties.name: Mission San José
properties.summary: 1797. Staging base for Central Valley expeditions.

properties.name: Mission San Rafael
properties.summary: 1817. Anti-Russian buffer. 83 days after Hagemeister treaty. Founded without a boat.

properties.name: Mission San Francisco Solano (Sonoma)
properties.summary: 1823. Northernmost mission. Altimira: "you will all be Russians."

properties.name: Pueblo de San José
properties.summary: 1777. First civilian settlement in Alta California.

properties.name: Villa de Branciforte (Santa Cruz)
properties.summary: 1797. Third and last pueblo. Named for Viceroy Branciforte. Planned as strategic settlement near Santa Cruz mission.

properties.name: Santa Eulalia (Cordelia)
properties.summary: ~1824. Mission Solano farming outpost — house for padre, corral, neophyte in charge. Suisun Valley.

properties.name: Port Rumiantsev (Bodega Bay)
properties.summary: ~1807. RAC fur-collecting base. First representative: Tarakanov. Permanent.
properties.notes: Anchorage in Bodega Harbor; the precise landing/port site is approximate.

properties.name: Fort Ross (Metini)
properties.summary: 1812. Ivan Kuskov. 95 Russians, ~80 Aleut. 12 guns, 3 falconets. No Spanish opposition.

properties.name: Farallon Islands Sealing Station
properties.summary: ~1812. 18 mi off SF coast. Seasonal Aleut camps. Unchallenged.

properties.name: Kostromitinov Ranch
properties.summary: 1833. Russian River near Willow Creek. ~100 acres wheat by 1841.
properties.notes: Exact ranch site debated (Osborn); descriptions place it on the south side of the Russian River near its mouth.

properties.name: Khlebnikov Ranch
properties.summary: 1833. Upper Salmon Creek valley, ~5 mi E of Bodega. Largest ranch — bakery, forge, tobacco shed.

properties.name: Chernykh Ranch
properties.summary: ~1833. Near Graton (Purrington Creek). First vineyard in Sonoma County.
